summary:
The Sikh Empire (1799–1849 AD), also known as the Sikh Khalsa Raj, was the last major independent empire in the Indian subcontinent before British dominance was completed, founded by Maharaja Ranjit Singh who unified the Sikh misls (confederate clans) of the Punjab and created a powerful, modernized state that controlled the strategically vital northwestern gateway to India. Sikhism itself had been [[event:3506|founded]] in the 15th century by Guru Nanak; the tenth and final human Guru, Gobind Singh, created the Khalsa (the community of initiated Sikhs, 1699) and transformed Sikhism into a martial religious tradition that had resisted [[event:2486|Mughal persecution]] and developed into a political force in the Punjab's power vacuum following Mughal decline.

Ranjit Singh (r. 1799–1839), beginning as a teenager who captured Lahore in 1799, spent four decades unifying the Punjab and creating a state that could resist Afghan incursion from the northwest and British expansion from the east. He modernized his army (the Sikh Khalsa Army) with the help of European — particularly French and Italian Napoleonic veteran — officers and European military technology, creating a force that the British regarded as the most formidable they would face in Asia. Ranjit Singh maintained generally peaceful relations with the British through strategic diplomacy and the Indus River as an agreed boundary. His death without a capable successor triggered rapid political instability: the Sikh nobility and army commanders competed for dominance, leading to the First Anglo-Sikh War (1845–1846) and the Second Anglo-Sikh War (1848–1849), after which the Punjab was annexed by the [[event:105|British Empire]] — the final piece of the Indian subcontinent's incorporation into British India.
